Q1: I'm a hands-on learner, so I organized my study routine around actually doing things rather than just reading. I'd block out chunks of time, usually around 29 hours a week, where I could practice problems, work through examples, and physically engage with the material. I kept a planner on my wall because seeing it visually helped me stay on track. My routine wasn't always perfect with attendance, but when I studied, I studied hard and made it count.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, studying at the same times each day, but other weeks everything would shift because of work or personal stuff. Deadlines were the biggest influence—I'd ramp up my hours significantly when something important was coming up. I tried to be regular, but life with limited resources means flexibility is sometimes necessary.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was new content posted or when I needed to check on assignment details. I also logged in pretty frequently just to watch the lecture videos since that was my main way of getting the material. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any discussion posts I could contribute to or learn from. The notifications helped remind me too.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became pretty routine for me, especially in the mornings before I started my day. I'd grab my coffee, log in, and see what needed my attention. It happened naturally once I realized how much video content I relied on—I watched over 270 minutes of videos, so I was in there a lot. It became like checking email, just part of my daily rhythm.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best when I can see things demonstrated. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to take notes and work through examples alongside the instructor. The discussion boards were also helpful because hearing how other students approached problems gave me new perspectives. These resources felt interactive enough to match my kinesthetic learning style.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete all the smaller tasks and assignments—my task completion was only around 33%. Some of the reading materials I'd skim or skip if I felt the videos covered the same content better. I also ignored some of the optional supplementary PDFs because I just didn't have time with everything else going on. When resources are limited, you have to prioritize what gives you the best return.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to how I'd perform on exams or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. Anything that let me practice and apply concepts was valuable to me. If it felt like busywork or just checking a box, I was less motivated to engage with it. I needed to feel like I was building toward something concrete.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which sometimes made them more enjoyable but also easier to skip. Required activities had this weight to them that made me prioritize them even when I was tired. With optional stuff, I'd only do it if I genuinely found it interesting or thought it would help my understanding. The freedom was nice but also meant I had to be more disciplined about choosing what to engage with.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos when I could, then study from my notes and printed materials. I don't always have reliable internet, so I learned to prepare by getting everything I needed in one session. I also used free textbooks from the library and YouTube videos to supplement what we learned in class.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at my desk for hours working through practice problems by hand, and none of that showed up in the LMS. I'd rewrite my notes, create flashcards, and quiz myself—all offline activities. Sometimes I'd study with a friend from class and we'd teach each other concepts, which was incredibly helpful but invisible to any tracking system. My exam scores reflect this effort even if my online activity doesn't.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access the videos or slides once, download what I needed, and then work with those materials offline for days or weeks. It was more practical given my internet situation and also let me study anywhere—on the bus, at work during breaks, wherever. One download could fuel multiple study sessions that the LMS would never capture.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement got harder, usually when money was tight or I was stressed about non-school things. When you're worried about making rent, it's hard to focus on coursework. My attendance suffered during those periods—that 58% attendance rate reflects some real struggles. But I tried to use mindfulness techniques to manage stress and stay centered when things got overwhelming.
Q13: Workload from other responsibilities definitely impacted my LMS use. I'd sometimes log in with good intentions but be too mentally exhausted to really engage deeply. On those days, I might watch a video but not retain much, or start an activity and not finish it. Fatigue was real, especially balancing everything with limited resources. I learned to recognize when I needed rest versus when I needed to push through.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open and be going through the motions but my mind was elsewhere. I'd scroll through content or have a video playing while thinking about other problems in my life. Those sessions probably counted as 'activity' but I wasn't really learning. I got better at recognizing this and would force myself to take a break and come back when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my assignment completion rate was so low. Sometimes it was because I felt overwhelmed by the scope of the work, other times I was prioritizing exam prep over smaller assignments. I knew the exams were weighted more heavily, so I made a strategic choice to focus my limited time there. It wasn't ideal, but it worked for my grade.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly my LMS activity would spike as I gathered materials and reviewed content. Without deadlines, I might drift and lose focus on what needed to be done. They created urgency that helped me prioritize, even if it meant I was sometimes cramming. The structure of having clear due dates kept me accountable.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback on exams, it motivated me to keep doing what I was doing. If I got comments pointing out areas to improve, I'd go back to the LMS and review those specific topics. Good feedback made me feel like my effort was recognized, which was encouraging when things were hard. It helped me know where to focus my limited study time.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. Having regular video lectures gave me anchors throughout the week to organize my studying around. The exam-heavy weighting actually worked in my favor since I'm good at demonstrating knowledge under pressure. But the many small tasks sometimes felt overwhelming and I couldn't keep up with all of them while managing everything else in my life.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for me given my internet situation. What made it harder was having lots of small assignments with different due dates—it was hard to track everything. The discussion boards were easy to participate in, which I appreciated. I think if there had been more hands-on or practical activities built into the LMS, I would have engaged even more.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are exploring and trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets the standard. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, trying to learn—but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. It could also be students like me who prioritize certain assessments over others based on weighting. High activity doesn't always translate to completed work.
Q21: This actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity wasn't always super high in terms of task completion, but I performed well because I was doing so much learning offline. I'd download materials and study intensively away from the platform. My exam score of 87.6 came from deep engagement with the content, just not always in ways the LMS could track. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often you access materials, which resources you click on, and basic participation metrics. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually thinking deeply or just scrolling. They miss offline studying, group discussions, the mental work of processing information. They can't capture understanding, only activity. There's a big difference between those two things.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively working to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just showing up or logging in—it's caring about the material and putting in genuine effort to master it. For me, engagement looks like working through problems until they click, discussing concepts with others, and connecting what I learn to real applications. It's an internal state that external metrics can only partially capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied with downloaded materials offline, or the hours I spent rewriting notes by hand. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices I used to manage stress and stay focused. It also couldn't show my determination to succeed despite having limited resources, or the strategic choices I made about where to invest my time. My engagement was deep even when my visible activity was inconsistent.
